desc:
This underground lake measures nearly twelve yards from one wall to the other.
It is nearly perfectly round, and a cascade of shimmering water falls from above
and splashes into the lake. The water is pretty clear in the upper part of the
lake, but around two, three yards below the surface, it gets darker and dirtier,
making it impossible to see the bottom. A small, crystal clear stream rushes out
of a narrow tunnel to the east and flows on through a lower, but broader one to
the west.
name: The Underground Lake

desc:
The narrow tunnel becomes wider to the west, and the water rushes to that
direction. It is just some two feet deep and so clear that it nearly emits its
own, light blue light. The ground is smooth and mostly flat, whereas moisture
makes the walls shimmer slightly. To the east, the tunnel seems to have caved in
years ago, and to the south someone created carvings in the stone.
name: A Small Underground Stream

desc:
The tunnel is quite low while the walls, ceiling and floor have been polished by
the rushing water. From the ceiling, dark green algae hang into the water and
soft moss grows in spots at the upper part of the walls. Further west, the
tunnel gets even lower and finally, the rock touches the ripples and the fast
flowing stream continues underwater.
name: A Stream in a Low Tunnel

desc:
The rock is smoothly polished by the permanent current. Some low plants, growing
on the bottom of this stream, move their tendrils in the flow of the water, and
little, yellow dots shimmer on their long, flexible leaves. The tunnel gets
narrower but also much higher in the southern part.
name: An Underground Stream

desc:
While the walls on the sides could be touched by stretching the arms, the bottom
and ceiling of this stream are barely visible in the dark. Yet, it can be made
out that the floor seems to be covered with dark soil and an occasional plant
with large, nearly perfectly round leaves.
name: A Narrow and Deep Underground Stream

desc:
As the underground stream becomes narrow and very low, the current is very
strong in this place. In this current, it is hard to figure out where bottom,
ceiling or side are. The walls, however, are slick and napless, and only very
few, thin algae are able to grow on the bare rock. In the far end of the sight,
the tunnel gets even narrower, and the stream rushes into a black hole to the
south.
name: A Rushing Underground Stream

desc:
It looks as though a streambed had once passed through here, though now it is
diminished and only a slight trace of it along the ground can be noticed. Many
fine grains of sand cover most of the ground here along with tiny pebbles, a
mixture of various colours. The grass along the west and east side of the faded
streambed is bright green, as though it really flourished near the water at one
time long ago. Near the south end of the old streambed, young blades of grass
have grown, but it is not nearly as thick as the surrounding patches.
name: Diminished Streambed

desc:
The streambed appears to have once led here from the west, and after a slight
curve it headed in a southbound direction. There are several plants trying to
emerge from the dried and cracked soil of the dried-up bed, but it would appear
they are struggling to survive here. Some bright green grass has grown thick to
the north, where it looks like the plains expand.
name: Old Streambed

desc:
The plains stretch from here, showing an assortment of grasses, wildflowers, and
small bushes of many different colours. A large stone stands almost in the
centre of the plain, light grey in colour, with a rough texture to it. Several
branches, all being very thin and splintered, are scattered around along the
ground.
name: Great Plains

desc:
The grassland here seems to continue to the south, though to the west, a few
trees stand barely discernible. The soil is broken up, with crisp blades of
grass growing in large clumps all over. Small mounds are rising out of the
earth, giving this landscape a distinct appearance.
name: Rough Grassland

desc:
Short, stunted grass covers this area into austere embrace. Even the land is
arid and barren, with its grass growing dark green. Some holes of various small
rodents can be seen all around the landscape, and almost non-existing trails
connect the burrows to each other. There are some old fences piled up along the
northeast side of the plain, and some large boulders stand in the southeastern
parts.
name: Bare Plain

desc:
The slightly rolling country here is filled with stunted hummocks and tussocks.
Some brushwood, mainly heather and some various herbs grow here. The ground
appears pretty dry, while the growth is strong and stable. A couple of large
rocks and circular-shaped stones are scattered around on the ground. To the
south, a slight rise in the earth can be noticed.
name: Stunted Hummocks

desc:
In the centre of the plains is a large knoll, emerged slightly from the earth.
Grass has fully covered the knoll, along with some white flowers that have grown
upon it. There are large stones at the southern end, which were probably some
type of a marker long ago, but have since crumbled and toppled over into a large
pile of debris. Long-stemmed wildflowers and tall blades of grass cover the
largest portion of debris, making it almost unnoticeable.
name: Steep Knoll

desc:
The flat field here consists of extremely dry ground. Some remains of fences
built long ago, made of some huge, dry logs, can still be seen around. Now their
skeletal forms are the sole guard of this long abandoned area. Some half-buried
bones are the only other reference to what this land might have formerly been
used for.
name: Ancient Lea

desc:
This part of the cavern is filled with smoke and other strong aromas floating in
the air. The stone floor is covered with a thick and soft carpet, which is
dirtied by all possible blemishes. Pieces of crude furniture, some wrecked and
some undamaged, share the floor space in disarray. Above a fireplace in the
corner, the cavern ceiling seems to disappear out of sight.
name: Smelly Chamber

desc:
This dark cavern opens as wide as high, enclosing all of the surroundings to
deep shadows. The basement rock along the ground is flooded with all sorts of
rubbish and food leftovers, which could be fresh marks of the inhabitants. Some
light can be seen further into the darkness. A small stream of water flows
onwards along the ground, only to disappear into a small hole.
name: Smelly Chamber

desc:
The threshold is long, though narrow, and leading toward a building that lies to
the north, with an oaken door marking its entrance. Thick, dark wood was used to
create the threshold, and a glossy finish was placed on it. The ceiling was
created with the same kind of wood, but at the southern end of it, there is a
red banner that dangles down. The ground is bare of any debris, materials, or
even grass, with only dry earth to walk over. The threshold is large enough so
that it appears to block any sunlight or rough weather from entering.
name: Threshold to a Shelter

desc:
This shelter was obviously reconstructed over time from its original build, as
some of the wood used for the walls is new compared to the rest. It has a glossy
coating upon it, while the old portions appear to have started to splinter due
to age. The western part of the ceiling is solid wood as well, while the rest is
covered with a thick tarp along one side of the shelter. Large wooden beams with
some engravings support the solid part of the roof, but the other side lacks
them. Especially the north-eastern portion of the roof looks extremely aged and
buckled. Large sheets of wood cover the floor, with an old rug lying upon them.
name: A Reconstructed Shelter

desc:
This roof covers a very old structure of some kind, even though the boards are
quite decayed and splintered in several different places. On the southeastern
side of the roof here, a dark-coloured tarp can be noticed, probably tied there
to cover a gaping hole. The roof steeply slants up to the west, while the
northern part seems easier to reach. Looking down from the roof, a rocky dale
runs to the south from this building.
name: On a Decaying Roof

desc:
The boards used for the roof here are very old, cracked in several places, and
already well into a decaying state. The roof rises up on a high slant to the
west, and would be too steep to climb to the other side. The boards along the
upper portion of that slant are even more decayed than those right here. The
roof offers a view of the surrounding dale but there is no visibility to the
fields above. A ladder is set against the northern wall of the dale, leading
upwards to a grassy ledge.
name: On a Decaying Roof

desc:
Short, strong grass covers the ground here. Some ancient stumps and boles have
broken through the grassbed, revealing the remains of some destroyed forest. A
single tree is still remaining, raising its naked trunk up to the heights. It
may have stood here branchless and leafless for ages, but still offers some
shelter to various small animals such as birds and insects.
name: Lone Tree in the Plains

desc:
Various bushes grow here, rising knee-high and covering the dry ground below
them. The brush has long and slender branches, extending out from the earth. A
few thorns which seem to be rather sharp and very short in length can be noticed
upon these branches.
name: Thorny Brush

desc:
Thick and strong brush covers the area, growing in all shapes and sizes. Nearly
hidden by the brushwood, a soft slope eases down into a vague sink in the land,
barely noticeable for an inattentive observer. Many tiny rills and small holes
in the surrounding ground yet indicate that the water from rainfalls steadily
flows into the dip, eroding the vicinity and slowly but steadily filling it.
name: Shallow Dip in the Brush

desc:
The landscape here is dominated by wildly sprawling shrubs and brush. On the
wind-beaten ground, various rocks are uncovered, protruding through the thin
layer of soil. The air is light, enriched by the spicy smell of the bushes which
nearly conceals the elusive scent of herbs and dirt carried along by the wind.
name: Wild Brush

desc:
The dry lands are filled with golden grass, grasping through the thin layer of
soil on the stony ground with their stems, leaving it cracked. At some places
the grass grows to the height of a fully grown man's waist, and yet, at in other
places it has lost the battle against the hard ground, leaving it barren and
only covered with a few patches of moss.
name: Grassy, Cracked Ground

desc:
Large patches of golden grass is growing here, now and then being interrupted by
solitary and small clusters of trees. The shimmering grass predominates the
landscape as the soil seems to be somewhat unyielding, but compared to what can
be seen to the south, it would do well for any farmer willing to bestow it.
name: Field of Grass

desc:
The grass here sparkles with an impressive variety of colours. While only a few
fully green straws can be seen, there are all shades and sizes of golden,
yellow, brown, grey and orange grass. In the setting sun, this field of colours
would have one face, while in the rising sun, an entirely different one would be
visible. Downfall is obviously plentiful to feed this variety of plants.
name: Lush Grassland

desc:
The flat surface of a steep cliff rises high to the north, looming over the
lower, grass-covered ground. Many flowers with a short stem and bulbous,
triangular blossom bloom here for most of the year, painting the dull landscape
in tones of violet, blue, pink and white. Among them, where the flowers grow
densest, there rests an old, mostly weathered stone with inscriptions on it.
name: South of a Steep Cliff

desc:
In most parts of this forest, the bushes and shrubs have grown that high and
thick that the only glimpse that can be caught of trees are their stems and
trunks if they come in the range of one's hands. Since not much light from the
sun reaches down here, most of the bushes consist of wooden and knobby branches
or remains of old, died and withered shrubs. Yet, many of the bushes have
developed thin, light brownish leaves for which the fractions of light reaching
down here are sufficient.
name: Intertwined Paths in the Forest

desc:
A few paths can be seen before they vanish somewhere in the thick underbrush
below the thick leaves and branches of the trees. The birds above and squirrels
near the ground have no problem with all these shrubs, trunks, bushes and dense
vegetation. But anything bigger than them has to find and force its own path.
For many years, this forest has grown to become the impenetrable thicket it is
today.
name: Intertwined Paths in the Forest

desc:
The trees have dense crowns which block most of the light of day or night, and
the shrubbery is thick and thorny. The ground is uneven, and at many spots,
little holes or depressions lurk below the deep grass. At some spots, where the
bigger bushes grow less dense, low plants with light yellow leaves and small,
green berries try to fight for their place in this heavily overgrown forest.
name: Intertwined Paths in the Forest

desc:
An eerie quietness dominates these woods. The near-reverent silence is broken
only sparingly by the soft rustle of young leaves. Only a few bushes grow
between the high trees. All around, there are slight and subtle signs of
habitation to be spotted. In a bush, some furs have been stuck on poles and form
a low roof, only big enough for someone to sleep under it. Nearby, a low shed of
branches has been built, while again a few steps away, some jars of clay hang in
the branches of a lower tree. A sheltered fireplace hides well behind two
smaller rocks. It seems as if someone has distributed everything needed for a
living within this copse of trees.
name: The Copse of Trees

desc:
Dirt covers the stony ground of this old corridor. It seems to have once been
furnished but the wood has collapsed into rotten heaps long ago. Decayed remains
of old tapestries and shields hang on the walls. Everything down here is covered
with layers upon layers of old, dusty cobwebs. Where the corridor bends to the
east, a few broken beams of stone hang down. A dark hole is visible high above
on the northern wall, constantly dripping moisture and tiny pebbles.
name: Cobwebbed Corridor

desc:
Thick, dusty cobwebs cover the walls and ceiling of this small chamber. Only a
closer inspection reveals rotten furniture below layers of webs, dust and dirt.
The floor is made of large flagstones and many seem broken or at least sunken in
and askew.
name: A Cobwebbed Chamber

desc:
Once this chamber seems to have been quite big, yet the larger part to the north
has collapsed as have most of the walls. The last leftovers of beams of stone
hang from the ceiling and most of the large tiles on the walls and floor are
broken and cracked. Clear, cold water now fills the entire chamber. Remains of
decoration such as shields, tapestries or weapons can be seen floating near all
of the walls but everything is too soaked and decayed to be of any use.
name: Underwater Chamber

desc:
The beams supporting this corridor have rotted away long ago and the walls and
the ceiling have collapsed, leaving a narrow passage filled with rocks, dirt and
soil. A small runlet of water makes the floor wet and has started to polish the
fallen stones. It flows down towards the eastern end of the corridor which seems
to have completely filled with water. Far above on the western wall, the
entrance to a dark tunnel is vaguely visible.
name: Collapsed Underground Corridor

desc:
This place seems to be more like a small alcove than any tunnel or actual
cavern. The walls are crowded with shelves, which are carrying various junk and
garbage. The footing here is filled with piles of some rotting food supplies and
fragments of containers which have fallen off from the shelves.
name: Storage Chamber

desc:
In this small and very high cave, all the cracks in the bottom have been filled
with sand, and the walls have been straightened out with clay. High up, there
are a few cracks left, which let in a few rays of light when the sun or the moon
is up. Hammocks made of fine, silvery cords hang near one wall, and a small
table has been built in a corner. Below the table, there is a big pile of
firewood. The walls, which are painted with lively colours to resemble a
prospering forest, bring life to this cave, making perfectly clear that this
underground place was not built by Dwarves or Men, but by Elves.
name: An Ancient Elven Hideout

desc:
A cramped tunnel opens into a small cavity. In its corners, there lie straw and
moss, placed as primitive resting quarters. In the centre, there is a rather
large hatch, covering a hole or a tunnel leading down. Sounds from the lands
outside only pass through the thick brush-walls as dampened whispers. The air
could be fresh, but a scent of blood and depreciating flesh lies above the smell
of leaves and wood.
name: Cavity Enclosed by the Brush

desc:
The smell of rotting meat and blood becomes more intensive further down in the
hole. A cave with numerous holes and cavities in the walls has been dug here by
removing the scarce supply of soil and dirt, uncovering a natural cave with
walls of rock. Bones and rotting corpses have been hung in one of the holes in
the rear wall, while the others are filled with straw. Next to the biggest hole,
a table and a kind of throne have been built from heavy branches and long bones.
name: The Orkish Commander's Hideout

desc:
The hall is nearly fifteen feet high and perfectly round. A dozen pillars stand
next to each other along the outer wall and each one bears a darkened coal pan
near the top. Even if the room looks truly ancient, even if most tiles and
pillars have at least cracks or have started to crumble and everything is
covered with a thick layer of white dust, the coals still burn with a magical
fire and spread a little light and heat. Below the dust, the floor is covered
with numerous shattered skeletons and smashed equipment. In the eastern wall
there is a completely collapsed and blocked passage, while behind a rusted grate
on the northern wall, a narrow shaft winds upwards into the darkness.
name: A Round Hall

desc:
A narrow path leads into a tunnel of dense, thorny bushes. The opening in the
twigs is bordered by thick thorns and branches. Forcing a path through the brush
in either direction would quickly prove impossible. Many smaller, broken twigs
can be seen on the ground and on the bushes left and right, indicating someone
or something walks along this path quite regularly.
name: Tunnel in the Brush

desc:
A large beech tree stands upon a small hill, the only point of recognition for
miles, as the surrounding landscape is filled with the same small bushes and
random vegetation. Thick, grey roots have surfaced over five feet away from the
tree, covered with many small fungi.
name: Lone Beech Tree

desc:
Innumerable bushes of varying sizes grow here, almost turning this area into a
jungle. Small twigs litter the ground surrounding them, many of them broken. The
brushwood is dense in some places, with roots appearing out of eroded earth. The
earth twists downwards to the south.
name: Brush Maze

desc:
The passageway leading down is covered by various sized pieces of gravel and
tiny pebbles. It is extremely steep, leading further beneath the mound. Due to
the passageway being slanted at such a great angle, without any bumps or
grooves, the footing is dangerous because of all the loose pieces of gravel. It
appears that further down there is a junction of some kind in the shape of a
fork, with two dark holes leading in different directions. The ceiling is quite
high at this point, and there seems to be another tunnel entrance on the eastern
wall, too far above to be reached.
name: Steep Gravel Passageway

desc:
Thick patches of moss grow on the ground, encircling the mighty trunks of the
trees which are coloured in green with a few tones of brown. Vines travel up the
trees, hiding themselves in the leafy branches above. The undergrowth is thick,
causing travel through to be quite difficult when on foot. Debris is scattered
all over the ground, most of it being dried-up leaves and broken branches. An
aged looking tower is camouflaged by the forest to the north. There are no paths
leading to this tower and the forest has almost engulfed it. Vines have scaled
their path up the tower walls as well and small strands of ivy are draped over
the top of the door frame.
name: Dense Forest near a Tower

desc:
Surrounded by large, dark trees, a nearly perfectly round place opens up here.
Thick vines with strong twines grow up the trees and connect them to each other
so that they appear like a green wall. Despite their form, there is no sign of
the trees having been arranged by any other means than nature. However, there
are signs of the opening being used as a stable. A wooden construction reaching
high above the ground, consisting of four poles covered with wooden planks,
serves as a roof to protect the mounts from rain. Three smaller but massive
poles beside three wooden feeders mark the places for each mount to be bound to.
Nearby the feeders, there is a large dunghill, consisting of both older and
quite fresh dung.
name: Secluded Opening in the Forest

desc:
The tower walls are made of black stone laid in a circular fashion. Each stone
appears to have been carved almost flawlessly, while white mortar was used to
seal them together tightly. The mortar is not very bright in contrast due to
dirt and dust settled upon the walls along the years. The frame which embraces
the entrance door into the tower is made of a white-speckled stone and is of an
unrivalled design. Along the wall are several candleholders, and the eastern
wall has a staircase which begins its climb upward in a spiralling manner. It
was made of the same black stone, with the exception of white, flat stone
overlays highlighting each step. White stone pillars scale up along the
staircase in a few select places.
name: Black Stone Tower

desc:
The staircase ascends into the top of the watch tower from here, and descends
back to the entrance below. Candleholders are hung on the wall aligned with the
spiral staircase, each giving their own glint of light from a white candle that
has been placed into the holder. Black stone was used to make the stairs, and
overlaying each stair is a flat and thin white-coloured stone. There are a few
white stone pillars climbing upwards against the staircase, helping to keep its
place tightly against the tower walls. The pillars come to an end at the
ceiling, adding support to the large sheet of black slate.
name: Spiral Staircase

desc:
The tower belvedere graces those on watch with visibility of the area which
surrounds the tower. The walls are of black stone, and a large sheet of
black-grooved slate lies as the flooring of the belvedere. An opening in the
slate turns into a spiral staircase, leading down to the base of the tower. In
the centre stands a low table made of a dark wood. There is a steel basin
sitting on this table, oval in shape, with a handle on each side. The ceiling is
made of another massive sheet of black slate, similar to the material used for
the floor. Four circular openings were carved into the tower walls and lined
with an iron frame to stabilise the surrounding stones.
name: Belvedere of the Tower

desc:
The corridor is not well lit, and what light does exist, is very dim. A black
stone was used in the making of this underground corridor, while the flooring is
a solid, large piece of black slate. The floor is not entirely flat, and the
slate has a glossy appearance to its finish. Tall stands of black steel line the
corridor, with openings at the top for placing large torches to add some light
into the gloom.
name: Black Stone Corridor

desc:
The corridor stretches ahead, crafted from black stone. The floor, a single,
imposing slab of black slate, adds an element of sleekness to the passage.
Despite the unevenness of the floor. Towering stands made of black steel flank
the corridor, each equipped with openings at the top to hold large torches.
name: Black Stone Corridor

desc:
Impeccably crafted, the walls of the checkpoint consist of black stone bricks,
showcasing their sturdy construction. Similar in material yet distinct in shape,
the floor is adorned with individual tiles. Towards the western end of the
chamber, the checkpoint culminates in a massive black slate wall.
name: Underground Checkpoint

desc:
An underwater river pulls its contents southward through a narrow tunnel,
enveloping them in a claustrophobic embrace. The murky waters and relentless
rush flow through the confined passage of black stone, emanating the strength
and power of a mighty river. Resisting the current would guide one back to solid
ground, away from the watery depths.
name: Murky Tunnel
